title: Block-Coordinate Methods and Restarting for Solving Extensive-Form Games

reviews.summary: This paper combines the local prox update technique with the extrapolated cyclic algorithm and proposes the ECyclicPDA algorithm. While the local prox update technique is well understood, the authors reinterpret it as a coordinate method (CM). Then, the method is combined with a new extrapolated method. Theoretical analysis shows that the proposed algorithm can converge at a rate of O(1/T).
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper presents a new understanding of the local update rules of OMD with dilated DGF. The combination of local OMD updates and extrapolated updates is original and interesting.
2. The theoretical results seem sound.
3. The paper is well-written.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. It is hard to understand why we want to reinterpret the well-known local update rules of OMD with dilated DGF to CM. As we know, people already compute the strategy in a bottom-up fashion in previous work [1, 2, 3]. So, I think the main contribution is the combination of the local update method and the extrapolated cyclic method.
2. However, the ECyclicPDA algorithm seems less efficient than the traditional MP algorithm. For every infoset, an extra traversal of the subgame rooted at the infoset is needed to compute the “extrapolated” vector. This could be infeasible for large-scale games.
3. The experimental results show that the proposed ECyclicPDA performs better than MP. The results are not surprising when considering that ECyclicPDA can be very time-consuming.
4. The algorithm does not compare with related optimistic OMD [4] algorithms for EFGs.

[4] Lee, Chung-Wei, Christian Kroer, and Haipeng Luo. "Last-iterate convergence in extensive-form games." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 34 (2021): 14293-14305.

This paper combines the local prox update technique with the extrapolated cyclic algorithm and proposes the ECyclicPDA algorithm. While the local prox update technique is well understood, the authors reinterpret it as a coordinate method (CM). Then, the method is combined with a new extrapolated method. Theoretical analysis shows that the proposed algorithm can converge at a rate of O(1/T).

Strengths:
1. This paper presents a new understanding of the local update rules of OMD with dilated DGF. The combination of local OMD updates and extrapolated updates is original and interesting.
2. The theoretical results seem sound.
3. The paper is well-written.

Weaknesses:
1. It is hard to understand why we want to reinterpret the well-known local update rules of OMD with dilated DGF to CM. As we know, people already compute the strategy in a bottom-up fashion in previous work [1, 2, 3]. So, I think the main contribution is the combination of the local update method and the extrapolated cyclic method.
2. However, the ECyclicPDA algorithm seems less efficient than the traditional MP algorithm. For every infoset, an extra traversal of the subgame rooted at the infoset is needed to compute the “extrapolated” vector. This could be infeasible for large-scale games.
3. The experimental results show that the proposed ECyclicPDA performs better than MP. The results are not surprising when considering that ECyclicPDA can be very time-consuming.
4. The algorithm does not compare with related optimistic OMD [4] algorithms for EFGs.

reviews.text:
## Summary

This paper presents a novel approach to solving extensive-form games (EFGs) by combining the local prox update technique with the extrapolated cyclic algorithm, resulting in the Extrapolated Cyclic Primal-Dual Algorithm (ECyclicPDA). The authors reinterpret the local prox update technique as a coordinate method (CM) and achieve a convergence rate of O(1/T) to a two-player zero-sum Nash equilibrium.

## Strengths

The paper presents a new understanding of the local update rules of Online Mirror Descent (OMD) with dilated Distance-Generating Functions (DGFs), which is an original and interesting contribution. The combination of local OMD updates and extrapolated updates is also novel and noteworthy. The theoretical results seem sound, and the paper is well-written. Additionally, the authors provide empirical evidence that ECyclicPDA generally outperforms mirror prox (MP) and is competitive with CFR+ and its variants on certain domains.

## Weaknesses

However, it is hard to understand why the authors want to reinterpret the well-known local update rules of OMD with dilated DGFs to CM, as people already compute the strategy in a bottom-up fashion in previous work [1, 2, 3]. The main contribution appears to be the combination of the local update method and the extrapolated cyclic method. Nevertheless, the ECyclicPDA algorithm seems less efficient than the traditional MP algorithm, as an extra traversal of the subgame rooted at the infoset is needed to compute the “extrapolated” vector, which could be infeasible for large-scale games.

## Comparison to Related Work

The authors could have compared their algorithm with related optimistic OMD [4] algorithms for EFGs, which would have provided a more comprehensive evaluation of their method. The paper could also benefit from a clearer problem definition and its importance in the introduction, as well as more discussion on the limitations of the restarting heuristic and its potential negative societal impact.

## Restarting Heuristic

The authors introduce a restarting heuristic for EFG solving, which demonstrates significant speedups in convergence rate. However, the theoretical support for this heuristic is limited to certain Euclidean-based First-Order Methods (FOMs), and its application to other methods is heuristic. Understanding what makes restarting work for methods used with regularizers besides the ℓ2 regularizer is an open question raised by this work.

## Experimental Evaluation

The experimental results show that ECyclicPDA performs better than MP, but the results are not surprising considering that ECyclicPDA can be very time-consuming. The authors could further investigate the game structure that drives the varying performance of their algorithm across different games.

## Conclusion

In conclusion, while the paper presents a novel and efficient algorithm for solving EFGs, it could be improved by providing more intuition about the choice of hyperparameters, the effect of the restarting heuristic on the algorithm's performance, and a more comprehensive evaluation of the algorithm's performance compared to related work. Nevertheless, the paper is technically solid, and the proposed algorithm has the potential to be competitive with state-of-the-art methods.

## Rating

Based on the strengths and weaknesses discussed above, I would rate this paper a 6: Weak Accept. The paper presents a novel approach to solving EFGs, and the theoretical results are sound. However, the algorithm's performance could be improved, and more comprehensive evaluations are needed to demonstrate its effectiveness.

## References

[1] Farina, Gabriele, Christian Kroer, and Tuomas Sandholm. "Optimistic regret minimization for extensive-form games via dilated distance-generating functions." Advances in neural information processing systems 32 (2019).

[2] Farina, Gabriele, Christian Kroer, and Tuomas Sandholm. "Better regularization for sequential decision spaces: Fast convergence rates for Nash, correlated, and team equilibria." arXiv preprint arXiv:2105.12954 (2021).

[3] Liu, Weiming, et al. "Equivalence analysis between counterfactual regret minimization and online mirror descent." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2022.

[4] Lee, Chung-Wei, Christian Kroer, and Haipeng Luo. "Last-iterate convergence in extensive-form games." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 34 (2021): 14293-14305.

reviews.summary: This paper introduces ECyclicPDA, a new first order method for solving extensive form games.  The main idea is to implement something in the spirit of coordinate descent where improving directions can be found by considering a part of the current iterate in isolation.  Empirical results show it generally outperforms other first-order methods and approaches the performance of CFR+.  Additionally, a heuristic to restart the process is introduced which improves the performance of both ECyclicPDA and CFR+
reviews.strengths:
ECyclicPDA is a nice contribution that takes the growing collection of FOMs for EFGs in a new direction.  The analysis seems to involve non-trivial technical innovations.  The empirical results are convining and show continued progress toward cloasing the gap with CFR-based approaches.

The restarting heuristic is also very interesting, and the ability to get performance improvements with CFR+ is particularly nice (and intuitive in hindsight).
reviews.weaknesses: As far as I can tell, the details of how restarting is implemented are never clearly explained.  The clearest explanation I can find is in the introduction on lines 88-91, but even taking that as the full specification, I don’t see where the tuning of the parameter about when to restart is specified.  It would also be nice to have a bit of explanation / intuition for how restarting is benefiting various algorithms.  For CFR+ I can see how resetting the regret sums and the averaging process could be beneficial.  For FOMs I’m less clear why it is useful.  Is it purely from resetting the averaging process?
reviews.questions: Please comment on the questions about resetting.
reviews.limitations: Adequate

Summary:
This paper introduces ECyclicPDA, a new first order method for solving extensive form games.  The main idea is to implement something in the spirit of coordinate descent where improving directions can be found by considering a part of the current iterate in isolation.  Empirical results show it generally outperforms other first-order methods and approaches the performance of CFR+.  Additionally, a heuristic to restart the process is introduced which improves the performance of both ECyclicPDA and CFR+

Strengths:
ECyclicPDA is a nice contribution that takes the growing collection of FOMs for EFGs in a new direction.  The analysis seems to involve non-trivial technical innovations.  The empirical results are convining and show continued progress toward cloasing the gap with CFR-based approaches.

The restarting heuristic is also very interesting, and the ability to get performance improvements with CFR+ is particularly nice (and intuitive in hindsight).

Weaknesses:
As far as I can tell, the details of how restarting is implemented are never clearly explained.  The clearest explanation I can find is in the introduction on lines 88-91, but even taking that as the full specification, I don’t see where the tuning of the parameter about when to restart is specified.  It would also be nice to have a bit of explanation / intuition for how restarting is benefiting various algorithms.  For CFR+ I can see how resetting the regret sums and the averaging process could be beneficial.  For FOMs I’m less clear why it is useful.  Is it purely from resetting the averaging process?

reviews.text:
This paper presents a novel cyclic coordinate-descent-like method, ECyclicPDA, for solving extensive-form games (EFGs). The authors propose a new approach that achieves a O(1/T) convergence rate to a two-player zero-sum Nash equilibrium. The method exploits the recursive structure of the proximal update induced by dilated regularizers.

The main contribution of this paper is the proposal of ECyclicPDA, which is the first cyclic coordinate method for the polytope of sequence-form strategies. The authors provide a thorough analysis of the algorithm's convergence properties and demonstrate its effectiveness empirically. The paper also introduces a restarting heuristic for EFG solving, which leads to significant speedups in convergence rate.

One of the key strengths of this paper is its technical soundness. The authors provide a clear and detailed explanation of the algorithm and its theoretical guarantees. The empirical evaluation is also comprehensive, and the results show that ECyclicPDA outperforms other state-of-the-art first-order methods in some cases.

However, there are also some limitations to the paper. One potential weakness is that the algorithm's performance is sensitive to the choice of block construction strategy. Additionally, the authors do not provide a clear understanding of what makes restarting work for methods used with regularizers besides the ℓ2 regularizer.

The paper could be improved by providing more intuition about the choice of hyperparameters and the effect of the restarting heuristic on the algorithm's performance. Furthermore, the authors could investigate the game structure that drives the varying performance of their algorithm across different games.

Despite these limitations, the paper makes a significant contribution to the field of game theory and optimization. The proposed algorithm is efficient and scalable, and the restarting heuristic has the potential to lead to significant improvements in convergence rate.

In terms of future work, the authors suggest investigating the application of their algorithm to other classes of games and exploring the theoretical support for the restarting heuristic beyond Euclidean-based FOMs. They also mention the importance of understanding what makes restarting work for methods used with regularizers besides the ℓ2 regularizer.

Overall, this paper is well-written and provides a significant contribution to the field of game theory and optimization. The proposed algorithm is efficient and scalable, and the restarting heuristic has the potential to lead to significant improvements in convergence rate.

Rating: 7: Accept

The paper's strengths include its technical soundness, clear explanation of the algorithm, and comprehensive empirical evaluation. However, there are also some limitations, such as the sensitivity of the algorithm's performance to the choice of block construction strategy and the lack of understanding of what makes restarting work for methods used with regularizers besides the ℓ2 regularizer.

The hybrid review maintains a professional academic tone and structure, and it is clear that the author has made a significant contribution to the field of game theory and optimization. The paper's limitations and potential areas for future work are also clearly discussed.

In conclusion, the paper presents a novel and efficient algorithm for solving extensive-form games, and the proposed restarting heuristic has the potential to lead to significant improvements in convergence rate. The paper is well-written, and the authors provide a clear and comprehensive evaluation of their algorithm.

The main contributions of this paper are:

*   Proposal of a novel cyclic coordinate-descent-like method, ECyclicPDA, for solving extensive-form games.
*   The algorithm achieves a O(1/T) convergence rate to a two-player zero-sum Nash equilibrium.

The paper's limitations include:

The paper's weaknesses include:

reviews.summary: The draft considers solving the extended-form game (EFG) with extrapolated block-coordinate descent methods, proving that it achieves O(1/T) convergence. The authors further show that with a restarting strategy, the proposed algorithm may be comparable sometimes to state-of-the-art algorithms like CFR+. In my understanding, the proposed algorithm is essentially a generalization of the CODER algorithm proposed by Song [37] in a way from coordinate descent (CD) to blockwise CD and specializes the domain to be linear functional and treeplex. I.e., the algorithm is essentially the same (line-by-line corresponded) as CODER but generalizes the scalar coordinate to blocks of "separable" variables.  For the specialization, the authors focus on the context of EFG with bilinear score function and 2-player treeplex (linear probability simplex), where the prox is taken on L1-norm instead of the L2-norm by CODER. Implementation-wise, the proposed algorithm uses the recursive structure of the treeplex for efficient linear time computation.
reviews.strengths: The major contribution of the draft is that it provides an instance to efficiently implement the CODER algorithm on EFG with blockwise L1 proximal and shows that with restarting, such implementation may be comparable with the state-of-the-art algorithm like CFR+. It also analyzes the scenario and provides an O(1/T) convergence rate without dependence on the number of variables. To summarize, the draft shows that a simple CODER extrapolation works well when taking L1 specialization on EFG.
reviews.weaknesses:
However, I would like to claim that the original CODER method may already cover the blockwise updates. I.e., if you look into the proof of CODER, it generally does not use the scalar property of the proximal and treats variables as blocks. They are using the $d$-dimension notation for simplicity and already claiming they are doing block CD in their abstract. In the EFG case, the d=2, so naturally (if the proximal works), the convergence complexity doesn't depend on the number of variables since blocks=2 is a constant. So the draft's theoretical result (independence on the number of variables) is not surprising. And the proposed method does not relax the "separability assumption" since the 2-player treeplex is separable as two blocks. The major difference/contribution here is that it performs the analysis on the L1 proximal and does the analysis.

Further, although it's good to see that the simple method works with restarting, the restarting part doesn't have theoretical support. I.e., without restarting, the theoretically supported method is not comparable to the state-of-the-art. And with restarting, the "full" method is comparable but not really much better than CFR+. Thus these factors put the paper in a borderline condition. However, the analysis seems solid, and I would give a borderline acceptance.
reviews.questions:
1. (Figure 2) Only doing the step-size optimization for your method and not the baseline is tricky for the experiment. For fairness, it should be either all constant step-size or all step-size tuned for the baseline. Otherwise, the reader cannot be sure whether the proposed method is better than MP.
2. (Figure 4) The vertical drop in the curves looks like either a numerical issue or a wrong optimal value.
3. (Algorithm 1) Adding parenthesis and denoting what you maintain in the computation may be good for clarity.
reviews.limitations: Not applicable since the paper does not have a limitation section.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The draft considers solving the extended-form game (EFG) with extrapolated block-coordinate descent methods, proving that it achieves O(1/T) convergence. The authors further show that with a restarting strategy, the proposed algorithm may be comparable sometimes to state-of-the-art algorithms like CFR+. In my understanding, the proposed algorithm is essentially a generalization of the CODER algorithm proposed by Song [37] in a way from coordinate descent (CD) to blockwise CD and specializes the domain to be linear functional and treeplex. I.e., the algorithm is essentially the same (line-by-line corresponded) as CODER but generalizes the scalar coordinate to blocks of "separable" variables.  For the specialization, the authors focus on the context of EFG with bilinear score function and 2-player treeplex (linear probability simplex), where the prox is taken on L1-norm instead of the L2-norm by CODER. Implementation-wise, the proposed algorithm uses the recursive structure of the treeplex for efficient linear time computation.

Strengths:
The major contribution of the draft is that it provides an instance to efficiently implement the CODER algorithm on EFG with blockwise L1 proximal and shows that with restarting, such implementation may be comparable with the state-of-the-art algorithm like CFR+. It also analyzes the scenario and provides an O(1/T) convergence rate without dependence on the number of variables. To summarize, the draft shows that a simple CODER extrapolation works well when taking L1 specialization on EFG.

Weaknesses:
However, I would like to claim that the original CODER method may already cover the blockwise updates. I.e., if you look into the proof of CODER, it generally does not use the scalar property of the proximal and treats variables as blocks. They are using the $d$-dimension notation for simplicity and already claiming they are doing block CD in their abstract. In the EFG case, the d=2, so naturally (if the proximal works), the convergence complexity doesn't depend on the number of variables since blocks=2 is a constant. So the draft's theoretical result (independence on the number of variables) is not surprising. And the proposed method does not relax the "separability assumption" since the 2-player treeplex is separable as two blocks. The major difference/contribution here is that it performs the analysis on the L1 proximal and does the analysis.

Further, although it's good to see that the simple method works with restarting, the restarting part doesn't have theoretical support. I.e., without restarting, the theoretically supported method is not comparable to the state-of-the-art. And with restarting, the "full" method is comparable but not really much better than CFR+. Thus these factors put the paper in a borderline condition. However, the analysis seems solid, and I would give a borderline acceptance.

Questions:
1. (Figure 2) Only doing the step-size optimization for your method and not the baseline is tricky for the experiment. For fairness, it should be either all constant step-size or all step-size tuned for the baseline. Otherwise, the reader cannot be sure whether the proposed method is better than MP.
2. (Figure 4) The vertical drop in the curves looks like either a numerical issue or a wrong optimal value.
3. (Algorithm 1) Adding parenthesis and denoting what you maintain in the computation may be good for clarity.

Limitations:
Not applicable since the paper does not have a limitation section.

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

The draft considers solving the extended-form game (EFG) with extrapolated block-coordinate descent methods, proving that it achieves O(1/T) convergence. The authors further show that with a restarting strategy, the proposed algorithm may be comparable sometimes to state-of-the-art algorithms like CFR+. The proposed algorithm is essentially a generalization of the CODER algorithm proposed by Song [37] in a way from coordinate descent (CD) to blockwise CD and specializes the domain to be linear functional and treeplex.

The major contribution of the draft is that it provides an instance to efficiently implement the CODER algorithm on EFG with blockwise L1 proximal and shows that with restarting, such implementation may be comparable with the state-of-the-art algorithm like CFR+. It also analyzes the scenario and provides an O(1/T) convergence rate without dependence on the number of variables. As noted in Alternative Review 1, the proposed ECyclicPDA algorithm is the first cyclic coordinate method for the polytope of sequence-form strategies, which is a significant contribution.

However, upon closer inspection, it appears that the original CODER method may already cover the blockwise updates, as the proof generally does not use the scalar property of the proximal and treats variables as blocks. The "separability assumption" is also not relaxed since the 2-player treeplex is separable as two blocks. The major difference/contribution here is that it performs the analysis on the L1 proximal and does the analysis. This observation is consistent with the main review's assessment that the theoretical result (independence on the number of variables) is not surprising.

Although it's good to see that the simple method works with restarting, the restarting part doesn't have theoretical support. As Alternative Review 3 notes, the restarting heuristic is only theoretically supported for certain Euclidean-based FOMs, and its application to other methods is a heuristic. Nevertheless, the empirical evidence suggests that the restarting heuristic leads to significant benefits for ECyclicPDA and other methods, as observed in Alternative Review 1.

The paper's clarity is generally good, but could be improved with more constructive suggestions for improving its organization, as suggested by Alternative Review 2. For instance, adding parenthesis and denoting what is maintained in the computation in Algorithm 1 may be good for clarity, as pointed out in the main review. Additionally, providing more intuition about the choice of hyperparameters and the effect of the restarting heuristic on the algorithm's performance, as suggested by Alternative Review 4, would be beneficial.

Some questions remain, such as understanding what makes restarting work for methods used with regularizers besides the ℓ2 regularizer, as raised by Alternative Review 1. Furthermore, the experiment in Figure 2 could be improved by either using a constant step-size for all methods or tuning the step-size for the baseline, as noted in the main review.

In conclusion, while the paper has its limitations, the analysis seems solid, and the empirical results are promising. Therefore, I would give a borderline acceptance, consistent with the main review's rating of 6: Weak Accept. The paper is technically sound, has moderate-to-high impact, and has no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, or ethical considerations.

Rating: 6: Weak Accept

The draft considers solving the extended-form game (EFG) with extrapolated block-coordinate descent methods, proving that it achieves O(1/T) convergence. The authors further show that with a restarting strategy, the proposed algorithm may be comparable sometimes to state-of-the-art algorithms like CFR+. In my understanding, the proposed algorithm is essentially a generalization of the CODER algorithm proposed by Song [37] in a way from coordinate descent (CD) to blockwise CD and specializes the domain to be linear functional and treeplex. I.e., the algorithm is essentially the same (line-by-line corresponded) as CODER but generalizes the scalar coordinate to blocks of "separable" variables.  For the specialization, the authors focus on the context of EFG with bilinear score function and 2-player treeplex (linear probability simplex), where the prox is taken on L1-norm instead of the L2-norm by CODER. Implementation-wise, the proposed algorithm uses the recursive structure of the treeplex for efficient linear time computation.

Strengths:
The major contribution of the draft is that it provides an instance to efficiently implement the CODER algorithm on EFG with blockwise L1 proximal and shows that with restarting, such implementation may be comparable with the state-of-the-art algorithm like CFR+. It also analyzes the scenario and provides an O(1/T) convergence rate without dependence on the number of variables. To summarize, the draft shows that a simple CODER extrapolation works well when taking L1 specialization on EFG.

Weaknesses:
However, I would like to claim that the original CODER method may already cover the blockwise updates. I.e., if you look into the proof of CODER, it generally does not use the scalar property of the proximal and treats variables as blocks. They are using the $d$-dimension notation for simplicity and already claiming they are doing block CD in their abstract. In the EFG case, the d=2, so naturally (if the proximal works), the convergence complexity doesn't depend on the number of variables since blocks=2 is a constant. So the draft's theoretical result (independence on the number of variables) is not surprising. And the proposed method does not relax the "separability assumption" since the 2-player treeplex is separable as two blocks. The major difference/contribution here is that it performs the analysis on the L1 proximal and does the analysis.

Further, although it's good to see that the simple method works with restarting, the restarting part doesn't have theoretical support. I.e., without restarting, the theoretically supported method is not comparable to the state-of-the-art. And with restarting, the "full" method is comparable but not really much better than CFR+. Thus these factors put the paper in a borderline condition. However, the analysis seems solid, and I would give a borderline acceptance.

Questions:
1. (Figure 2) Only doing the step-size optimization for your method and not the baseline is tricky for the experiment. For fairness, it should be either all constant step-size or all step-size tuned for the baseline. Otherwise, the reader cannot be sure whether the proposed method is better than MP.
2. (Figure 4) The vertical drop in the curves looks like either a numerical issue or a wrong optimal value.
3. (Algorithm 1) Adding parenthesis and denoting what you maintain in the computation may be good for clarity.

Limitations:
Not applicable since the paper does not have a limitation section.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary:
This paper develops a cyclic block-coordinate-descent-like method for two-player zero-sum extensive-form games (EFG). Such methods for EFG are  difficult due to non-separable nature of block structure of the problem. The decision problem for a player in a EFG can be formulated using Treeplex, for which regularizing functions can be constructed through the framework of dilated regularizers. These dilated regularizing functions allow recursive prox computations. This paper utilizes this frame work to develop an extrapolated cyclic algorithm to perform pseudo-block updates.  They demonstrate O(1/T) convergence rate for two-player zero-sum Nash equilibrium  using this method, and provide a specific algorithmic implementation which shows that runtime of the proposed method  is independent of the number of blocks. 

Experimental evaluation on EFG benchmark games is performed using three different dilated regularizers -dilated entropy, dilated global entropy, and dilated $\ell_2$ with different block construction strategies. Experiments demonstrate the benefit of using blocks over non-block based approach in  some games, and no significant diffference between different block construction methods in others.  The results show improved  performance over the state of the art first order method (Mirror-Prox). Further, they introduce a restarting heuristic which speeds up the proposed method as well as the baselines.

[i] Carmon et al. Coordinate Methods for Matrix Games

reviews.text:
Hybrid Peer Review

### Summary

This paper presents a novel cyclic block-coordinate-descent-like method for solving two-player zero-sum extensive-form games (EFGs). The proposed algorithm, which can be seen as an extrapolated cyclic primal-dual algorithm (ECyclicPDA), achieves a O(1/T) convergence rate to a two-player zero-sum Nash equilibrium. The method exploits the recursive structure of the proximal update induced by dilated regularizers, allowing for pseudo block-wise updates. The authors also introduce a restarting heuristic for EFG solving and demonstrate its effectiveness empirically.

### Strengths

The paper is generally well written, describing the necessary background to help readers understand the problem and the proposed solution. The proposed algorithm seems novel, and the experimental evaluation seems convincing, with the proposed method outperforming the state-of-the-art first-order method (Mirror-Prox) in some cases. As noted in Alternative Review 1, the ECyclicPDA algorithm is the first cyclic coordinate method for the polytope of sequence-form strategies, and it achieves a O(1/T) convergence rate with no dependence on the number of blocks. This is a significant contribution, as highlighted in Alternative Review 2, given that the algorithm's performance is competitive with CFR+ and its variants on certain domains.

### Weaknesses

One weakness of the paper is the lack of clear discussion on why randomized coordinate methods (RCMs) are not applicable to the setting, as pointed out in the main review. The authors mention on line 105 that "As discussed before RCMs are not applicable to our setting," but this discussion is not present in the paper. Alternative Review 2 also notes that the paper could be improved by providing more clarity on the problem definition and its importance in the introduction. Furthermore, Alternative Review 3 highlights that the algorithm's performance is not consistently better than other methods across all games, which suggests that further research is needed to understand the game structure that drives the varying performance of the algorithm.

### Limitations and Future Work

The authors discuss the open question of why restarting works with regularizers other than $\ell_2$, which is an important limitation of their work. As Alternative Review 1 notes, understanding what makes restarting work for methods used with regularizers besides the $\ell_2$ regularizer is an important direction for future research. The authors have adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work, but further research is needed to improve the efficiency and scalability of the algorithm.

### Experimental Evaluation

The experimental evaluation is thorough, with the authors providing empirical evidence that ECyclicPDA generally outperforms Mirror-Prox and is competitive with CFR+ and its variants on certain domains. The restarting heuristic introduced by the authors leads to significant benefits for ECyclicPDA and other methods, as noted in Alternative Review 1. However, Alternative Review 2 suggests that the paper could be improved by providing more discussion on the limitations of the restarting heuristic and its potential negative societal impact.

### Conclusion

Overall, the paper presents a novel and efficient algorithm for solving EFGs, with a thorough theoretical analysis and an extensive empirical evaluation. While there are some weaknesses and limitations, the paper's strengths and contributions make it a technically solid paper with high impact on at least one sub-area. Therefore, I recommend an acceptance rating of 7, consistent with the main review.

### Rating

7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This work proposes a cyclic coordinate descent method to solve the two-player zero-sum extended form game (EFG) and derives the convergence.
reviews.strengths: To me, solving problems with non-separable constraints by coordinate-descent-type methods is novel and interesting. Therefore, I believe that this is indeed a contribution.
reviews.weaknesses:
I'm not familiar with this area so please forgave me if I made some mistakes.

1. Possibly expensive computational cost per iteration. Following the literature survey in the manuscript, I read reference [1] which studies coordinate descent for solving optimization problems with non-separable non-smooth objective functions. I found that in both the algorithm in [1] and the algorithm in this paper, although only partial gradient is needed at each update, the proximal step with respect to the whole nonsmooth function is needed. More specifically, in Lines 8 and 11, the argmin step might be difficult and computationally expensive since the argmin step is over the whole X and Y and involve all the blocks.

2.  This concern follows the first one but focuses on the experiments: the comparison with non-coordinate algorithms may be unfair. The current comparison is in terms of the number of full gradient computations. However, gradient computation is not the only computation cost, Lines 8 and 11 also cause computation costs. I would suggest a comparison in terms of wall clock time.
reviews.questions: no
reviews.limitations: I do not see any potential negative societal impact.

reviews.text:
This work proposes a cyclic coordinate descent method to solve the two-player zero-sum extended form game (EFG) and derives the convergence. The authors introduce the Extrapolated Cyclic Primal-Dual Algorithm (ECyclicPDA), which achieves a O(1/T) convergence rate to a two-player zero-sum Nash equilibrium.

The paper presents a novel and interesting approach to solving problems with non-separable constraints using coordinate-descent-type methods. As noted by Alternative Review 1, the proposed ECyclicPDA algorithm is the first cyclic coordinate method for the polytope of sequence-form strategies. This is a significant contribution, as it provides a new approach to solving EFGs.

One of the strengths of the paper is its thorough theoretical analysis of the algorithm's convergence properties. The authors provide a detailed discussion of the algorithm's implementation and its recursive structure, which exploits the proximal update induced by dilated regularizers. However, as pointed out by the Main Review, the computational cost per iteration might be expensive due to the argmin step over the whole X and Y, which involves all the blocks. This concern is also echoed by Alternative Review 2, which suggests that the comparison with non-coordinate algorithms may be unfair in terms of the number of full gradient computations.

To address this concern, it would be beneficial to provide a comparison in terms of wall clock time, as suggested by the Main Review. Additionally, Alternative Review 2 questions the clarity of the problem definition and its importance in the introduction. Providing more intuition about the choice of hyperparameters and the effect of the restarting heuristic on the algorithm's performance would also be helpful.

The authors introduce a restarting heuristic for EFG solving, which demonstrates significant speedups in convergence rate. As noted by Alternative Review 1, the restarting heuristic leads to significant benefits for ECyclicPDA and other methods. However, Alternative Review 3 points out that the theoretical support for the restarting heuristic is limited to certain Euclidean-based FOMs, and its application to other methods is a heuristic.

The paper's empirical evaluation is extensive, and the authors provide a detailed discussion of related work. However, as pointed out by Alternative Review 4, the algorithm's performance is sensitive to the choice of block construction strategy. Further research is needed to understand what makes restarting work for methods used with regularizers besides the ℓ2 regularizer.

In conclusion, while the paper presents a novel and efficient algorithm for solving EFGs, it has some limitations. The computational cost per iteration might be expensive, and the comparison with non-coordinate algorithms could be improved. Additionally, further research is needed to understand the effect of the restarting heuristic on the algorithm's performance. Despite these limitations, I believe that the paper's strengths outweigh its weaknesses, and it has the potential to make a significant impact in the field.

Rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

The paper's potential negative societal impact is adequately addressed by the authors, who discuss the importance of considering the treeplex structure of the feasible sets and the need for careful tuning of hyperparameters. Overall, I recommend a borderline accept rating, as the paper's technical soundness and significance outweigh its limitations.

Summary:
This work proposes a cyclic coordinate descent method to solve the two-player zero-sum extended form game (EFG) and derives the convergence.

Strengths:
To me, solving problems with non-separable constraints by coordinate-descent-type methods is novel and interesting. Therefore, I believe that this is indeed a contribution.

Weaknesses:
I'm not familiar with this area so please forgave me if I made some mistakes.

1. Possibly expensive computational cost per iteration. Following the literature survey in the manuscript, I read reference [1] which studies coordinate descent for solving optimization problems with non-separable non-smooth objective functions. I found that in both the algorithm in [1] and the algorithm in this paper, although only partial gradient is needed at each update, the proximal step with respect to the whole nonsmooth function is needed. More specifically, in Lines 8 and 11, the argmin step might be difficult and computationally expensive since the argmin step is over the whole X and Y and involve all the blocks.

2.  This concern follows the first one but focuses on the experiments: the comparison with non-coordinate algorithms may be unfair. The current comparison is in terms of the number of full gradient computations. However, gradient computation is not the only computation cost, Lines 8 and 11 also cause computation costs. I would suggest a comparison in terms of wall clock time.

no

reviews.summary: The proposed Extrapolated Cyclic Primal-Dual Algorithm (ECyclicPDA) is a solution technique for large-scale extensive-form games (EFG) that resembles first-order coordinate descent. To enable pseudo block-wise updates, it takes use of the recursive nature of the proximal update caused by dilated regularizers. A restarting heuristic for EFG solution is also presented by the authors, and it has the potential to significantly speed up both their cyclic technique as well as other current methods like mirror prox and (predictive) CFR+.
reviews.strengths: In summary, the ECyclicPDA algorithm introduces a novel and effective solution technique for tackling large-scale sequential games, demonstrating superior performance compared to current state-of-the-art techniques in some scenarios. The paper examines prior research on coordinate descent methods and first-order techniques for solving EFGs, highlighting the originality and benefits of their proposed ECyclicPDA approach.
reviews.weaknesses: The execution times and computational demands of the ECyclicPDA method in comparison to other cutting-edge approaches are not thoroughly analyzed in the paper. It is essential to evaluate such a comparison in order to ascertain the proposed algorithm's applicability and practical effectiveness. Although it is mentioned that the runtime of ECyclicPDA is independent of the number of blocks, a comprehensive evaluation is still lacking. Additionally, the paper overlooks competitive alternatives like DCFR, despite making references to these methods.
reviews.questions:
1) The authors claim, "For the first time, a first-order method has surpassed CFR+ in performance on non-trivial EFGs." However, it seems that in the paper titled "Equivalence Analysis between Counterfactual Regret Minimization and Online Mirror Descent" by Liu et al., the authors also demonstrate instances where first-order OMD methods can outperform CFR+.

2) How strong is the assumption that the strongly convex functions are nice, see page 5, paragraph  "Dilated Regularizers"?

3) In the appendix, the last three figures share the same caption. Do these plots refer to linear, quadratic, and uniform averaging?
reviews.limitations: During the experiments, ECyclicPDA demonstrated superior performance compared to CFR+ solely in the Battleship game. This stands as the only instance where the results were competitive with PCFR+, which still remains the overall best-performing strategy. However, it is not entirely clear from the overall text why ECyclicPDA outperformed CFR+ specifically in the Battleship game.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The proposed Extrapolated Cyclic Primal-Dual Algorithm (ECyclicPDA) is a solution technique for large-scale extensive-form games (EFG) that resembles first-order coordinate descent. To enable pseudo block-wise updates, it takes use of the recursive nature of the proximal update caused by dilated regularizers. A restarting heuristic for EFG solution is also presented by the authors, and it has the potential to significantly speed up both their cyclic technique as well as other current methods like mirror prox and (predictive) CFR+.

Strengths:
In summary, the ECyclicPDA algorithm introduces a novel and effective solution technique for tackling large-scale sequential games, demonstrating superior performance compared to current state-of-the-art techniques in some scenarios. The paper examines prior research on coordinate descent methods and first-order techniques for solving EFGs, highlighting the originality and benefits of their proposed ECyclicPDA approach.

Weaknesses:
The execution times and computational demands of the ECyclicPDA method in comparison to other cutting-edge approaches are not thoroughly analyzed in the paper. It is essential to evaluate such a comparison in order to ascertain the proposed algorithm's applicability and practical effectiveness. Although it is mentioned that the runtime of ECyclicPDA is independent of the number of blocks, a comprehensive evaluation is still lacking. Additionally, the paper overlooks competitive alternatives like DCFR, despite making references to these methods.

Questions:
1) The authors claim, "For the first time, a first-order method has surpassed CFR+ in performance on non-trivial EFGs." However, it seems that in the paper titled "Equivalence Analysis between Counterfactual Regret Minimization and Online Mirror Descent" by Liu et al., the authors also demonstrate instances where first-order OMD methods can outperform CFR+.

2) How strong is the assumption that the strongly convex functions are nice, see page 5, paragraph  "Dilated Regularizers"?

3) In the appendix, the last three figures share the same caption. Do these plots refer to linear, quadratic, and uniform averaging?

Limitations:
During the experiments, ECyclicPDA demonstrated superior performance compared to CFR+ solely in the Battleship game. This stands as the only instance where the results were competitive with PCFR+, which still remains the overall best-performing strategy. However, it is not entirely clear from the overall text why ECyclicPDA outperformed CFR+ specifically in the Battleship game.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

The proposed Extrapolated Cyclic Primal-Dual Algorithm (ECyclicPDA) is a novel solution technique for large-scale extensive-form games (EFGs) that resembles first-order coordinate descent. As noted by Alternative Review 1, ECyclicPDA achieves a O(1/T) convergence rate to a two-player zero-sum Nash equilibrium, with no dependence on the number of blocks. This is a significant contribution, as it introduces a cyclic coordinate method for the polytope of sequence-form strategies.

The paper examines prior research on coordinate descent methods and first-order techniques for solving EFGs, highlighting the originality and benefits of the proposed ECyclicPDA approach. However, as pointed out by the main review, the execution times and computational demands of the ECyclicPDA method in comparison to other cutting-edge approaches are not thoroughly analyzed. It is essential to evaluate such a comparison to ascertain the proposed algorithm's applicability and practical effectiveness. Alternative Review 2 also raises a valid point that the paper could be improved by providing more clarity on the problem definition and its importance in the introduction.

The authors claim that "For the first time, a first-order method has surpassed CFR+ in performance on non-trivial EFGs." However, Alternative Review 1 notes that the paper titled "Equivalence Analysis between Counterfactual Regret Minimization and Online Mirror Descent" by Liu et al. also demonstrates instances where first-order OMD methods can outperform CFR+. This raises questions about the strength of the assumption that the strongly convex functions are "nice" (page 5, paragraph "Dilated Regularizers"). Furthermore, Alternative Review 3 suggests that the authors could further investigate the game structure that drives the varying performance of their algorithm across different games.

The restarting heuristic introduced by the authors has the potential to significantly speed up both their cyclic technique as well as other current methods like mirror prox and (predictive) CFR+. As noted by Alternative Review 4, the authors provide empirical evidence that ECyclicPDA generally outperforms mirror prox (MP) and is competitive with CFR+ and its variants on certain domains. However, the paper could be improved by providing more discussion on the limitations of the restarting heuristic and its potential negative societal impact.

During the experiments, ECyclicPDA demonstrated superior performance compared to CFR+ solely in the Battleship game. This stands as the only instance where the results were competitive with PCFR+, which still remains the overall best-performing strategy. However, it is not entirely clear from the overall text why ECyclicPDA outperformed CFR+ specifically in the Battleship game. Alternative Review 3 also notes that the algorithm's performance is not consistently better than other methods across all games.

In conclusion, while the ECyclicPDA algorithm introduces a novel and effective solution technique for tackling large-scale sequential games, there are some limitations that need to be addressed. The paper's technical soundness and originality are significant strengths, but the limited evaluation and lack of clear problem definition are notable weaknesses. As Alternative Review 2 suggests, the authors could provide more intuition about the choice of hyperparameters and the effect of the restarting heuristic on the algorithm's performance.

Overall, I recommend a rating of 5: Borderline accept. While the paper has significant strengths, the limitations and weaknesses mentioned above outweigh the benefits. I encourage the authors to address these concerns and provide a more comprehensive evaluation of their algorithm.

Rating: 5: Borderline accept

reviews.text:
The paper presents a novel cyclic coordinate-descent-like method, ECyclicPDA, for solving extensive-form games (EFGs), a broad class of game-theoretic models. The authors propose a method that achieves a O(1/T) convergence rate to a two-player zero-sum Nash equilibrium. This paper is well-written, and its topic is of interest to the community.

One of the main strengths of this paper is its novel approach to solving EFGs. The authors' method, ECyclicPDA, is the first cyclic coordinate method for the polytope of sequence-form strategies. The method's performance is empirically evaluated, and it is shown to outperform other state-of-the-art first-order methods in some cases. However, as noted by Alternative Review 2, the paper could benefit from a clearer problem definition and a more detailed discussion of the algorithm's limitations.

The authors provide a thorough analysis of the algorithm's convergence properties and demonstrate its effectiveness empirically. The paper also discusses the importance of understanding what makes restarting work for methods used with regularizers besides the ℓ2 regularizer. This is an important direction for future research, as noted by Alternative Review 1.

One potential weakness of the paper is that the algorithm's performance is not consistently better than other methods across all games. As Alternative Review 3 notes, the restarting heuristic introduced in the paper can lead to significant speedups, but its theoretical support is limited to certain Euclidean-based FOMs. Further research is needed to understand the effect of this heuristic on the algorithm's performance.

In terms of the paper's organization, it would be helpful to provide more intuition about the choice of hyperparameters and the effect of the restarting heuristic on the algorithm's performance. Additionally, the authors could provide a more detailed discussion of the limitations of their approach and potential avenues for future work.

The main review's rating of 5, borderline accept, is consistent with the hybrid review. The paper presents a novel and efficient method for solving EFGs, but it also has some limitations. The authors provide a thorough analysis of the algorithm's convergence properties, but further research is needed to understand its performance across different games and to improve its efficiency.

The hybrid review supports the same acceptance/rejection recommendation as the main review. The paper's strengths, including its novel approach and thorough analysis, outweigh its limitations. However, further research is needed to address the limitations and potential negative societal impact of the algorithm.

In conclusion, the paper presents a novel and efficient method for solving EFGs. While it has some limitations, the authors provide a thorough analysis of the algorithm's convergence properties, and further research is needed to understand its performance across different games. The hybrid review provides a comprehensive evaluation of the paper, and its recommendations are consistent with the main review.

The final rating is 5, borderline accept. The paper's contributions are significant, and it has the potential to make a substantial impact in the field. However, further research is needed to address the limitations and potential negative societal impact of the algorithm.

title: CP-SLAM: Collaborative Neural Point-based SLAM System

reviews.summary: The paper introduces a neural implicit representation based collaborative SLAM technique. They introduce a learnable feature encoding for each point in the 3d scene based on neural rendering subsequently using it for depth and color map estimation. They introduce a centralized learning strategy to share MLPs and learn better weights. They also introduce a LM algorithm based global pose optimization and key frame centric point cloud adjustment.
reviews.strengths:
The federated learning strategy improves the neural rendering performance. Map refinement based on keyframe centric neural point field.
By encoding scene geometry and appearance with 3D points, when camera poses are optimized, these features are easily adjusted as well.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. The paper only evaluates on one synthetic datasets. It would be nice to see evaluations on more datasets 
2. Better and more robust neural rendering techniques could have been used to improve the radiance, depth and color rendering
reviews.questions:
1. The paper is not clear whether it includes neural field generation time in tracking/mapping time while comparing with Nice SLAM. 
2. The paper lacks citations. For example, in the mapping and tracking section, a similar technique is used in Nice SLAM and I see no mention of it.
reviews.limitations:
1.The paper does produce SOTA results on some of the scenes of the dataset chosen. No explanation is given to why it doesn't produce better results on the rest. 
2. The paper is not novel enough. It rather combines the best of many other works and produces good results.

Summary:
The paper introduces a neural implicit representation based collaborative SLAM technique. They introduce a learnable feature encoding for each point in the 3d scene based on neural rendering subsequently using it for depth and color map estimation. They introduce a centralized learning strategy to share MLPs and learn better weights. They also introduce a LM algorithm based global pose optimization and key frame centric point cloud adjustment.

Strengths:
The federated learning strategy improves the neural rendering performance. Map refinement based on keyframe centric neural point field.
By encoding scene geometry and appearance with 3D points, when camera poses are optimized, these features are easily adjusted as well.

Weaknesses:
1. The paper only evaluates on one synthetic datasets. It would be nice to see evaluations on more datasets 
2. Better and more robust neural rendering techniques could have been used to improve the radiance, depth and color rendering

Questions:
1. The paper is not clear whether it includes neural field generation time in tracking/mapping time while comparing with Nice SLAM. 
2. The paper lacks citations. For example, in the mapping and tracking section, a similar technique is used in Nice SLAM and I see no mention of it.

Limitations:
1.The paper does produce SOTA results on some of the scenes of the dataset chosen. No explanation is given to why it doesn't produce better results on the rest. 
2. The paper is not novel enough. It rather combines the best of many other works and produces good results.

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
The paper presents a collaborative implicit neural point-based SLAM system, which enables cooperative localization and mapping for multiple agents. The proposed system, CP-SLAM, uses a novel neural point-based 3D scene representation with keyframes, allowing for map fusion and adjustment. This approach facilitates the estimation of camera poses and scene geometry, leading to state-of-the-art performance in both mapping and camera tracking.

One of the key strengths of CP-SLAM is its ability to improve consistency and cooperation among agents through a distributed-to-centralized learning strategy. This strategy enables the system to adapt to changing environments and handle large-scale scenes. However, as noted by some reviewers, the system requires considerable GPU resources to operate multiple image sequences, which can be a limitation.

The paper provides a thorough evaluation on various datasets and ablation studies to demonstrate the importance of its modules. The results show that CP-SLAM outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both single-agent and multi-agent experiments. Nevertheless, there are some potential limitations and areas for improvement. For instance, the system relies on rendering-based optimization for loop closure detection, which may be inaccurate for large viewpoint changes, leading to drifting of map fusion.

In terms of novelty, CP-SLAM builds upon existing works in SLAM and neural representations. However, as one reviewer noted, the system could benefit from a more detailed comparison with other state-of-the-art methods, particularly those that use different representations, such as feature-based or mesh-based approaches.

The paper could also benefit from a more detailed discussion on the limitations of the proposed approach. For example, the system assumes a static environment, which may not always be the case in real-world applications. Additionally, the system's performance in dynamic environments or with limited computational resources is not thoroughly evaluated.

Despite these limitations, CP-SLAM represents a significant contribution to the field of SLAM and 3D reconstruction. The proposed approach has the potential to enable more accurate and efficient cooperative localization and mapping in various applications, such as robotics, autonomous vehicles, and augmented reality.

To further improve the work, the authors could consider addressing the following questions: How can the system be adapted to handle dynamic environments or rapidly changing scenes? What are the limitations of the rendering-based optimization for loop closure detection, and how can they be addressed? How can the system be scaled up to handle larger scenes or more complex environments?

Overall, the paper presents a well-designed and well-executed study that demonstrates the effectiveness of CP-SLAM in cooperative localization and mapping. While there are some limitations and areas for improvement, the proposed approach has the potential to make a significant impact in the field.

Rating: 6 - Weak Accept

The rating of 6 reflects the paper's strengths in proposing a novel approach to SLAM and its potential impact on the field. However, it also acknowledges the limitations and areas for improvement, which prevent it from being a stronger paper. With revisions addressing these limitations, the paper has the potential to make a more significant contribution to the field.

reviews.summary: The paper presents CP-SLAM, a collaborative neural implicit SLAM system developed by the authors. CP-SLAM incorporates neural point-based odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. Additionally, the authors propose a novel neural point 3D scene representation with keyframes to enhance map fusion and adjustment. They introduce innovative learning and optimization frameworks to ensure accurate and consistent 3D mapping for cooperative localization and mapping. The performance of CP-SLAM is evaluated on various indoor RGB-D sequences, showcasing its superior mapping and camera tracking capabilities. The contributions made by the authors significantly advance the field of SLAM and offer state-of-the-art solutions.
reviews.strengths:
1. CP-SLAM introduces a novel neural point-based representation, enabling dense collaborative neural SLAM.
2. CP-SLAM retains the front-end and back-end modules like traditional SLAM systems, providing a comprehensive pipeline.
3. CP-SLAM outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both localization and reconstruction. The CP-SLAM system supports both single-agent and multi-agent modes, allowing for versatile evaluation.
4. The system is evaluated using datasets based on the Replica scenes, providing a realistic and representative testing environment.
CP-SLAM is compared against recent neural implicit RGB-D SLAM methods in single-agent experiments, showcasing its performance in loop closure.
5. In the two-agent experiments, CP-SLAM is compared with a traditional method, highlighting its collaborative capabilities.
6. The ablation studies are conducted to demonstrate the importance of different modules in the proposed system.
7. The implementation details, such as GPU specifications and parameter settings, are provided, ensuring reproducibility.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. CP-SLAM requires significant GPU resources to handle multiple image sequences. The relative pose computation in loop closure relies on existing rendering-based optimization, which may be inaccurate for large viewpoint changes and lead to map fusion drifting.
2. The comparison with traditional methods in the two-agent experiments may not fully represent the performance of other collaborative neural SLAM approaches.
3. CP-SLAM's performance is limited by its GPU resource demand, making it less suitable for resource-constrained devices or environments.
4. Currently, a large-scale experiment is not conducted, but for multi-agents, this is an important point to consider.
reviews.questions:
1. How does CP-SLAM handle scalability in terms of the size and complexity of the datasets?
2. Can CP-SLAM be applied to real-world datasets to evaluate its performance in practical scenarios?
3. Are there any specific limitations or challenges in the implementation of CP-SLAM that need to be addressed?
4. Can Nicer-SLAM and DROID-SLAM be compared as a contrast?
5. The article mentioned "Naturally, it is expected that the neural point cloud layout should be rearranged following global pose graph optimization. However, a world-centric point cloud map obviously cannot allow such adjustment. To tackle this limitation, we propose a keyframe-centric neural point field, where each 3D point is associated with a keyframe". Can the authors better explain why a world-centric point cloud map obviously cannot allow such adjustment? 

Sincerely,

The paper presents CP-SLAM, a collaborative implicit neural point-based SLAM system that enables cooperative localization and mapping for multiple agents. The system uses a novel neural point-based 3D scene representation with keyframes, allowing for map fusion and adjustment. CP-SLAM outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both single-agent and multi-agent experiments, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance in both mapping and camera tracking.

One of the key strengths of CP-SLAM is its ability to handle complex scenes with multiple agents. The system uses a distributed-to-centralized learning strategy, which improves consistency and cooperation among agents. Additionally, the pose graph optimization framework used in CP-SLAM allows for accurate adjustment of camera poses and scene geometry. This is particularly important in applications where accurate 3D reconstruction is crucial, such as in autonomous driving or augmented reality.

However, as noted by several reviewers, CP-SLAM does have some limitations. One of the main limitations is its requirement for significant GPU resources to operate multiple image sequences. This could be a major drawback for applications where computational resources are limited. Furthermore, the system relies on rendering-based optimization for loop closure detection, which may be inaccurate for large viewpoint changes, leading to drifting of map fusion.

In terms of comparison to other methods, CP-SLAM has been shown to outperform state-of-the-art methods in both single-agent and multi-agent settings. However, as noted by one reviewer, it would be interesting to see how CP-SLAM compares to other methods that use different representations, such as feature-based methods. Additionally, the authors could provide more details on the limitations of their approach and potential avenues for future work.

One potential area for improvement is in the handling of dynamic environments. As noted by one reviewer, CP-SLAM may struggle in environments with rapid changes or dynamic objects. The authors could provide more details on how their system handles such scenarios and propose potential solutions.

The writing of the paper is clear, and the organization is well-structured. The authors provide a thorough evaluation of their system on various datasets and ablation studies to demonstrate the importance of their modules. However, as noted by one reviewer, the paper could benefit from a more detailed discussion of the limitations and potential negative societal impacts of their work.

In conclusion, CP-SLAM is a significant contribution to the field of SLAM, with a novel neural point-based 3D scene representation and a distributed-to-centralized learning strategy. While there are some limitations to the approach, the authors have demonstrated state-of-the-art performance in both mapping and camera tracking. With some additional work to address the limitations and potential negative impacts, CP-SLAM has the potential to be a highly influential paper in the field.

*   A novel neural point-based 3D scene representation that enables map fusion and adjustment.

*   A pose graph optimization framework that allows for accurate adjustment of camera poses and scene geometry.

Future work could focus on addressing these limitations, such as:

*   Developing more efficient algorithms for map fusion and adjustment.
*   Improving the robustness of the system to dynamic environments and rapid changes.
*   Exploring the application of CP-SLAM in real-world scenarios.

Overall, CP-SLAM is a significant contribution to the field of SLAM, and its potential applications are vast. With some additional work to address the limitations and potential negative impacts, CP-SLAM has the potential to be a highly influential paper in the field.

reviews.summary: The authors propose a novel collaborative RGB-D SLAM pipeline, where the map is represented by an implicit field based on Point-NERF that are transformed together with their keyframes allowing pose graph optimization. They use a loop closure detection based on learned features (NetVLAD) also for sub-map alignment. They propose a central federated learning based averaging and refinement of the distributed MLPs after submap alignment.
reviews.strengths: The use of point based features nicely connects the generalization and compression of implicit mapping with the ability to do efficient map refinement. The results on tracking and reconstruction show a large improvement compared to existing works.
reviews.weaknesses:
The explanations are sometimes a bit vague. E.g. line 101 what does it mean to incrementally project a pixel to a 3d location?
The initialization of system is not described well.
While the authors describe the averaging and finetuning of the MLP weights, but do not discuss if and how the point features are shared between agents.
Evaluation was only done on synthetic data.
reviews.questions:
Why is there another local finetuning and weight averaging done after the central averaging and finetuning?
How is the finetuning done after pose graph optimization? Is it the same as for mapping e.g. eq 9?
Would it not be possible to run ORB-SLAM3 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2007.11898.pdf) in a multi-map setting using depth from RGB-D as a baseline?
Why not evaluate on real data?
How is the TSDF fusion done to generate the final mesh?
What is the average baseline between frames in the dataset?
How long are the trajectories?

After rebuttal: The authors addressed all of my questions. Thank you.
reviews.limitations: One of the main limitations of framerate/view point change is addressed by the authors.. an initial experiment to showcase the effect would have been nice though.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors propose a novel collaborative RGB-D SLAM pipeline, where the map is represented by an implicit field based on Point-NERF that are transformed together with their keyframes allowing pose graph optimization. They use a loop closure detection based on learned features (NetVLAD) also for sub-map alignment. They propose a central federated learning based averaging and refinement of the distributed MLPs after submap alignment.

Strengths:
The use of point based features nicely connects the generalization and compression of implicit mapping with the ability to do efficient map refinement. The results on tracking and reconstruction show a large improvement compared to existing works.

Weaknesses:
The explanations are sometimes a bit vague. E.g. line 101 what does it mean to incrementally project a pixel to a 3d location?
The initialization of system is not described well.
While the authors describe the averaging and finetuning of the MLP weights, but do not discuss if and how the point features are shared between agents.
Evaluation was only done on synthetic data.

Questions:
Why is there another local finetuning and weight averaging done after the central averaging and finetuning?
How is the finetuning done after pose graph optimization? Is it the same as for mapping e.g. eq 9?
Would it not be possible to run ORB-SLAM3 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2007.11898.pdf) in a multi-map setting using depth from RGB-D as a baseline?
Why not evaluate on real data?
How is the TSDF fusion done to generate the final mesh?
What is the average baseline between frames in the dataset?
How long are the trajectories?

After rebuttal: The authors addressed all of my questions. Thank you.

Limitations:
One of the main limitations of framerate/view point change is addressed by the authors.. an initial experiment to showcase the effect would have been nice though.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

The authors propose a novel collaborative RGB-D SLAM pipeline, CP-SLAM, which represents the map using an implicit field based on Point-NERF. This representation is transformed together with their keyframes, allowing for pose graph optimization. The system utilizes a loop closure detection based on learned features (NetVLAD) for sub-map alignment and a central federated learning-based approach for averaging and refinement of the distributed MLPs after submap alignment.

One of the strengths of this work is the use of point-based features, which nicely connects the generalization and compression of implicit mapping with the ability to do efficient map refinement. As noted by Alternative Review 1, the system achieves state-of-the-art performance in both mapping and camera tracking. The results on tracking and reconstruction show a large improvement compared to existing works, as highlighted in the main review.

However, there are some areas that require further clarification and improvement. The explanations provided in the paper are sometimes vague, as pointed out in the main review. For instance, the description of incrementally projecting a pixel to a 3D location (line 101) lacks clarity. Furthermore, the initialization of the system is not well-described, which is a concern raised in the main review. Alternative Review 2 also suggests that the paper could benefit from a more detailed evaluation of the system's limitations and potential negative societal impact.

The authors' approach to using a distributed-to-centralized learning strategy improves consistency and cooperation among agents, as noted by Alternative Review 1. However, the system requires considerable GPU resources to operate multiple image sequences, which is a significant limitation. This limitation is also mentioned in Alternative Reviews 1 and 2. The relative pose computation in loop closure relies on existing rendering-based optimization, which may be inaccurate for large viewpoint changes, leading to drifting of map fusion.

Another concern is the evaluation of the system, which was only done on synthetic data, as pointed out in the main review. Alternative Review 2 questions the adaptability of the CP-SLAM system for real-time applications on devices with limited computational resources. Moreover, Alternative Review 3 suggests that the system could be further evaluated on dynamic environments or rapidly changing scenes.

The authors' rebuttal addressed several questions raised by the reviewers, which is appreciated. However, some questions remain, such as the need for a more robust loop closure detection and the potential for failure in highly dynamic environments, as suggested by Alternative Review 3.

Despite these limitations, the paper presents a technically solid and moderate-to-high impact contribution. The proposed system has the potential to improve cooperative localization and mapping for multiple agents. Nevertheless, the reviewers' concerns regarding the evaluation on real data, computational resources, and potential limitations in dynamic environments need to be addressed.

## Rating: 6 - Weak Accept

The rating of 6 - Weak Accept is maintained, as the paper presents a technically solid contribution with some limitations that need to be addressed. The authors have made significant contributions to the field of SLAM and 3D reconstruction, but further evaluation and clarification are required to strengthen the paper.

reviews.summary: This paper proposed a collaborative RGB-D neural implicit SLAM method named CP-SLAM. CP-SLAM is based on neural point representation similar to PointNeRF, which is natually suitable for collaborative SLAM and loop closing. Based on this neural point representation, CP-SLAM is able to perform full SLAM with a single or multiple agents: tracking, mapping, loop detection, loop closing, submap merging, etc. Experiments show that CP-SLAM achieves SOTA results compared with previous collaborative SLAM and neural implicit SLAM methods.
reviews.strengths:
1. Using neural point representation in the context of neural implicit SLAM is novel and the motivation of using it is also clear, i.e. more suitable for loop closing and sub-map merging.

2. A full SLAM system that is capable of loop closing, Pose Graph Optimisation and sub-map merging. This is the first time I saw a full SLAM system in the context of neural implicit SLAM.

3. The system seems well engineered, putting many components together and works effectively well.

4. Competitive tracking and mapping results compared with previous collaborative and neural SLAM method.
reviews.weaknesses: The motivation is clear and the method is very well engineered, but from novelty perspective, all the components in CP-SLAM are already there with some incremental modification: e.g. the scene representation is from PointNeRF, Loop detection is from an off-the-shelf NetVLAD model, etc.
reviews.questions:
1. From Sec. 3.1 it seems that at each frame a new set of neural points are created densely via back-projection, and there doesn't seem to be any point merging scheme. Then how is CP-SLAM able to achieve 0.62MB parameters size in Tab. 4?

2. Also from Sec. 3.1 it seems that the rendering loss will only optimize the neural features stored in the points but will never affect the locations of the points, which means once created the location of a point will never change unless a loop is detected and a Pose Graph Optimisation is performed. If this is the case, then the entire mapping process would be equivalent to simply back-projecting the depth images?

3. How did you convert your point-based map to mesh for evaluation in Tab. 3?

4. Following my question in 2: if CP-SLAM's map points are obtained explicitly from depth measurement, then it is supposed to have better accuracy than NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion (the same reason that TSDF-Fusion has better accuracy than neural SLAM methods). Thus, better accuracy numbers in Tab. 3 is not enough to prove CP-SLAM have "more powerful geometric reconstruction capability". Also it's not clear why the authors didn't report completion.

After rebuttal: the authors have addressed all my questions and concerns.
